articles.title: Russian Hawks Urge Putin To Escalate War, Drop U.S. Talks As Ukraine Strikes Deep
articles.content:
莫斯科，6月26日（路透社）——乌克兰无人机袭击引发俄方强烈不满，俄方认为美国未能兑现促成结束战争的有利条款。俄罗斯强硬派正在敦促总统普京放弃外交手段，转而升级军事行动。

要求采取更强硬措施的呼声并非新鲜事。民族主义声音长期以来一直呼吁进行全面动员、摧毁基辅政府区、暗杀泽连斯基总统，以及打击欧洲无人机工厂。一些鹰派人士甚至呼吁克里姆林宫负责人考虑使用战术核武器。

但乌克兰本月发动的深入打击——目标是莫斯科、圣彼得堡和克里米亚，以及俄方称造成人员死亡的两次客运巴士袭击——使这些诉求更加尖锐和强烈。

分析师表示，日益激烈的言辞反映出人们对乌克兰无人机袭击的范围和影响越来越感到不安，也引发了一场更广泛的辩论：俄罗斯在广阔领土上如何既能保卫自己，又能在2022年发动的这场冲突中实现其战争目标。

民族主义寡头康斯坦丁·马洛费耶夫在上周乌克兰袭击导致莫斯科一座炼油厂起火后表示：“还需要发生什么才能让我们开始真正战斗？战争意味着不惜一切代价取得胜利。乌克兰人正处于战争中，所以他们正在倾尽全力战斗。”

他问道：“为什么我们不使用核武器？我们的先辈正是为了这个目的，以举国之力研发和储备了这些武器？”

放弃和平谈判的呼声

一些民族主义评论员敦促莫斯科采取他们所称的伊朗对抗美国的有效军事和外交策略。拥有超过65万粉丝的“战争痴迷”博客呼吁通过轰炸使乌克兰主要城市不适宜居住。其他人则表示，现在是放弃美国斡旋的和平谈判、追求彻底摧毁乌克兰国家的时候了。

拥有近9万粉丝的民族主义博主尤里·巴兰奇克在Telegram上表示：“泽连斯基政权对莫斯科发动系统性空袭是不可能的，除非得到华盛顿的批准。特朗普为什么要给泽连斯基开绿灯？答案很简单——伊朗抓住了特朗普的把柄，迫使他签署了一份屈辱的协议。”

巴兰奇克说：“现在他需要迅速找人出气……所以我们别无选择——要么我们压倒特朗普，要么他压倒我们。”

与克里姆林宫关系密切的消息人士表示，普京可以容忍这种言辞。他坐在一个经过26年精心构建的严格控制的政治体系顶端，民族主义博主需要遵守某些规则。

然而，分析师表示，此类言论仍可能通过煽动公众情绪和提高对更广泛军事行动的预期来使决策复杂化，尽管莫斯科仍希望为潜在的外交解决方案保持开放。

克里姆林宫抵制强硬派压力

到目前为止，克里姆林宫抵制了鹰派放弃谈判的呼吁，尽管三位政府高级官员本周表示，与美国的谈判毫无进展，并指责华盛顿没有落实去年在阿拉斯加举行的普京-特朗普峰会上提出的和平方案。

普京也避免支持民族主义者最极端的提议，尽管国防部4月份刻意公布了据称生产乌克兰无人机的几个欧洲国家工厂地址，这似乎是警告这些目标可能受到打击。

俄罗斯外交部上月也发出升级的信号，表示莫斯科打算对基辅的军事目标发动“系统性打击”。随后进行了更猛重的轰炸袭击，其中包括一座有千年历史的基辅修道院受损。

目前，普京似乎对当前战略充满信心。他周二告诉军事院校毕业生，俄罗斯即将占领乌克兰东部的康斯坦丁诺夫卡市，这是其控制顿巴斯地区攻势的一部分。

他还表示，欧洲那些对俄罗斯持敌视态度的政治力量很可能被其认为更合理的对手所取代。

普京说：“那些希望与我们恢复正常关系、停止这场无休止地寻求俄罗斯战略性失败的势力正在崛起。最终一切都会好起来的。”

（记者安德鲁·奥斯本  编辑：罗斯·拉塞尔）
articles.summary:
LOADINGERROR LOADING
&

MOSCOW, June 26 (Reuters) - Seething over Ukrainian drone strikes and angered by what they see as a failed U.S. promise to broker an end to the war on favourable terms, Russian hardliners are urging President Vladimir Putin to abandon diplomacy and escalate.

Calls for tougher measures are not new. Nationalist voices have long pressed for full mobilisation, the destruction of Kyiv’s government quarter, the assassination of President Volodymyr Zelenskiy and strikes on European drone factories. Some hawks have even called on the Kremlin chief to consider using tactical nuclear weapons.

Advertisement

But Ukraine’s deep strikes this month — targeting Moscow, St Petersburg and Crimea, as well as what Russia said were two deadly attacks on passenger buses — have sharpened and intensified those demands.

Analysts say the increasingly strident rhetoric reflects growing unease over the reach and impact of Ukrainian drone attacks, and a broader debate about how Russia — with its vast territory — can defend itself while still pursuing its war aims in a conflict it launched in 2022.

“What else needs to happen before we start fighting for real? War means victory at any cost. The Ukrainians are at war, so they’re fighting with everything they’ve got,” said Konstantin Malofeyev, a nationalist tycoon, after a Ukrainian strike set fire to a Moscow oil refinery last week.

Advertisement

“Why are we not using nuclear weapons, which our forebears developed and stockpiled with the full might of the nation precisely for this purpose?” he asked.

Calls to abandon peace talks

Some nationalist commentators have urged Moscow to adopt what they cast as Iran’s effective military and diplomatic tactics against the United States. The Obsessed by War blog, with over 650,000 followers, has called for rendering major Ukrainian cities unliveable through bombing. Others say it is time to abandon U.S.-brokered peace talks and pursue the outright destruction of the Ukrainian state.

Advertisement

“The start of systematic air strikes on Moscow by the (Ukrainian) junta would have been impossible without the go-ahead from Washington. And why did Trump give Zelenskiy such a green light? The answer is very simple – Iran had Trump by the balls, and he was forced to sign a humiliating agreement,” said nationalist blogger Yuri Baranchik, who has nearly 90,000 followers.

“Now he needs to take it out on someone quickly ... So we’ve got no choice – either we’ll get the better of Trump, or he’ll get the better of us,” Baranchik said on Telegram.

Sources close to the Kremlin say Putin can tolerate such rhetoric. He sits atop a tightly-controlled political system he has built over 26 years and nationalist bloggers need to conform to certain rules.

Advertisement

Analysts say, however, that such statements can still complicate decision-making by inflaming public sentiment and raising expectations of a more expansive military campaign, even as Moscow still wants to keep the door open for a potential diplomatic solution.

Kremlin resists hardline pressure

So far, the Kremlin has resisted the hawks’ calls to abandon negotiations, even though three senior government officials said this week that talks with the U.S. had gone nowhere and accused Washington of not following through on peace proposals it made at last year’s Putin-Trump summit in Alaska.

Putin has also avoided endorsing the nationalists’ most extreme proposals, although the Defence Ministry in April pointedly published the addresses of factories in several European countries it alleged made drones for Ukraine, in what appeared to be a warning they could be targeted.

Advertisement

Russia’s Foreign Ministry also signposted an escalation last month when it said Moscow intended to launch “systematic strikes” on military targets in Kyiv. Heavier bombing runs followed, including one in which a 1,000-year-old monastery in Kyiv was damaged.

For now, Putin appears confident in the current strategy. He told military academy graduates on Tuesday that Russia was close to seizing the city of Kostyantynivka in eastern Ukraine, as part of its push to control the Donbas region.

He also said political forces in Europe who were hostile to Russia looked likely to be eclipsed by rivals he cast as more reasonable.

Advertisement

“Those who want to restore normal relations with us, to stop this endless drive for a strategic defeat of Russia, are on the rise,” said Putin. “It’ll all work out in the end.”

(Reporting by Andrew Osborn Editing by Ros Russell)

Related
PoliticsRussiaUkraineworld newsNews

articles.title: U.S. Strikes Iran To Respond To Attack On Ship That Trump Says Violated Ceasefire
articles.content:
华盛顿（美联社）——美国周五对伊朗发动袭击，以回应前一天在霍尔木兹海峡发生的无人机袭击货船事件。伊朗这一挑衅行为被美国总统唐纳德·特朗普称为“愚蠢的违反停火协议行为”。

美国中央司令部表示，军事行动打击了伊朗的导弹和无人机阵地以及沿海雷达站点。

就在美国发动反击前不久，特朗普曾对记者说，美国是否会对无人机袭击作出回应，“你们会知道的”。

“我不喜欢他们昨天开火的事实，实际上是四发，”特朗普在白宫说，“他们有点不太一样。”随后他突然中断提问，记者被请出办公室。

英国军方周四表示，一艘集装箱船在阿曼海岸附近遭到炮弹袭击，此事件发生在伊朗威胁船只停止使用该航线数小时后。英国海上贸易运营中心表示，袭击未造成人员伤亡。

这一事态发展正值美国和伊朗谈判永久结束战争的关键时期。尽管伊朗与美国上周达成了临时协议，伊朗仍日益挑战该地区及美国对霍尔木兹海峡的控制权。

无人机袭击货船事件发生时，联合国一个海事机构本周正开始执行一项行动，通过沿阿曼海岸航行的替代路线将滞留船只移出海峡，而非穿越海峡中部。

国际海事组织在袭击发生后暂停了撤离行动，并周五表示，在获得其他船只不会遭到袭击的保证之前，不会恢复行动。

该机构秘书长阿塞尼奥·多明格斯表示，约有115艘船只在近几天得以离开海峡，目前仍有约500艘滞留在该区域。

美国和伊朗仍在谈判协议条款，包括确保船只通过这一关键海峡以及处理伊朗高浓缩铀库存问题。根据临时协议，双方有60天时间敲定细节。

货船袭击对航运构成考验

航运分析师表示，无人机袭击给原本不断增多的滞留船只最终离开海湾以及载运原油的油轮流量回升蒙上了阴影。

“霍尔木兹海峡商业信心扩大的一周遭遇了首次重大考验，”海洋数据公司Windward在社交媒体平台X上表示。该公司表示，尽管海峡在袭击后仍保持运营开放，记录到43艘次通行，“正常化进程已经放缓”。

周四无人机袭击前的周三，有78艘船只通过海峡，是战争开始以来的最高纪录，但仍低于战前每天130艘以上的平均水平。

根据海洋数据和分析公司劳氏情报列表的数据，至少有两艘油轮在试图通过联合国支持的阿曼近海路线过境时掉头返航，此前伊朗坚持船只只能使用德黑兰批准的航线。

劳氏周五表示，袭击发生后，仍有20多艘船只在海峡南部航线通行。

黎巴嫩和以色列迈出和平一步

以色列和黎巴嫩大使周五宣布了一项协议，描述为在以色列军队与黎巴嫩真主党武装组织之间数月冲突后迈向和平的一步。

黎巴嫩驻美国大使纳达·哈马德称这一框架是“让人民重返家园、让所有黎巴嫩人生活在和平、安全与繁荣中”的举措。

以色列总理本雅明·内塔尼亚胡称该计划是以色列的“重大成就”。

“最重要的是，首先，以色列将留在黎巴嫩南部的安全区，”他说，并补充说他们将留守直到真主党解除武装且不再对以色列构成威胁。

——

甘布雷尔发自阿拉伯联合酋长国迪拜。美联社记者本·芬利、乔希·博克（华盛顿）、大卫·麦休（德国法兰克福）和约翰·西维尔（俄亥俄州托莱多）对此报道有贡献。
articles.summary:
LOADINGERROR LOADING
&

WASHINGTON (AP) — The U.S. struck Iran on Friday to respond to a drone attack a day earlier on a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz, a provocation that U.S. President Donald Trump said was a “foolish violation” of the ceasefire.

U.S. Central Command said the military struck missile and drone locations and coastal radar sites in Iran.

Advertisement

The strikes came shortly after Trump told reporters, “You’ll find out,” whether the U.S. would response to the drone attack.

“I don’t like the fact that they took a shot yesterday, actually four of them,” Trump said at the White House shortly before the U.S. struck back. When asked why there would be strikes when Trump has insisted talks with Tehran are going well, Trump said of Iran: “They’re a little bit different.”

He then abruptly cut off questions and reporters were ushered out of his office.

The British military said on Thursday that a container ship was hit a projectile off the coast of Oman, coming hours after Iran threatened vessels to stop using the route. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations center said no injuries were reported.

Advertisement

The development came during a fragile time for the U.S. and Iran as they work to negotiate a permanent end to the war. Iran has increasingly challenged the region and the U.S. over its control of the Strait of Hormuz, even with the current interim deal it reached with the U.S. last week.

The attack on the cargo ship happened while a United Nations maritime agency was beginning an operation to move stranded ships out of the strait this week, using an alternative route, hugging the shores of Oman rather than sailing through the central part of the strait.

The International Maritime Organization halted the evacuations after the attack and said on Friday they won’t resume until there are guarantees that the other ships won’t be attacked.

Advertisement

About 115 ships were able to move out of the strait in recent days, leaving about 500 still in the area, said Arsenio Dominguez, the agency’s secretary-general.

The opening of the alternative passage through the strait was expected to relieve pressure on the world economy and remove Iran’s main source of leverage in ongoing peace talks with the U.S.

The U.S. and Iran are still negotiating terms of the deal, including issues such as getting ships through the key strait and addressing the future of Iran’s stockpile of highly enriched uranium. Under the interim deal, the two sides have 60 days to work out the details.

Advertisement

Cargo Ship Attack Poses A Test For Shipping

Shipping analysts said the drone strike cast a shadow over what had been a growing stream of trapped vessels finally leaving the Gulf and an increasing flow of tankers carrying crude oil.

“A week of widening commercial confidence in the Strait of Hormuz has hit its first significant test,” said marine data company Windward on X. It said that while the strait remains operationally open with 43 transits recorded after the incident, “the pace of normalization has slowed.”

Advertisement

On Wednesday before Thursday’s drone strike, 78 vessels transited the strait, the highest since the war began, although below the prewar averages of 130 or more per day.

At least two tankers reversed course while attempting to transit the strait on the U.N.-backed route near Oman after Iran insisted vessels use only the Teheran-approved routes, according to marine data and analytic firm Lloyd’s List Intelligence.

More than two dozen ships were still transiting the strait’s southern route after the attack, Lloyd’s said Friday.

Advertisement

Lebanon And Israel Make A Step Toward Peace

Ambassadors from Israel and Lebanon announced an agreement Friday described as a step toward peace following months of conflict between Israeli troops and the Lebanese militant group Hezbollah.

Nada Hamadeh, Lebanon’s ambassador to the U.S., called the framework a move toward “enabling our people to go back to their land and allowing all Lebanese to live in peace, security, and prosperity.”

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said the plan was a “great achievement” for Israel.

“The most important thing, first and foremost, is that Israel will remain in the security zone in southern Lebanon,” he said, adding that they will stay until Hezbollah is disarmed and no longer poses a threat to Israel.

Advertisement

___

Gambrell reported from Dubai, United Arab Emirates. Associated Press writers Ben Finley and Josh Boak in Washington, David McHugh in Frankfurt, Germany, and John Seewer in Toledo, Ohio, contributed to this report.

Related

Donald Trump

Politics

Iran

world news

Strait of Hormuz

U.S., Iran Reach Deal To End The War, Reopen Strait Of Hormuz
Trump's War In Iran Is A Staggering Failure On Nearly Every Level
Trump Made Sure To Have An Iran ‘Deal’ In Hand The G7. People Don’t Seem That Impressed.

articles.title: Iran Insists On Right To Control Shipping In Strait Of Hormuz After Ship Hit Near Oman
articles.content:
迪拜/伦敦，6月26日（路透社）——伊朗周五重申其对霍尔木兹海峡航运的控制权，并警告海湾国家不要站在美国一边。就在前一天，一艘在阿曼附近海域遭到袭击的船只，暴露了结束伊朗战争的初步协议有多么脆弱。

德黑兰方面回应称，美方与六个海湾国家发表的联合声明“干预性强、不负责任且具有挑衅性”，该声明拒绝接受伊朗坚持对过境船只收取通行费的主张。

伊朗外交部副部长卡齐姆·加里巴巴迪在社交媒体X上表示：“在模糊的安排、平行航线或不考虑伊朗作为沿海国家角色的决策框架下，霍尔木兹海峡的安全通行无法得到保障。”

周五油价进一步下跌，尽管外界对伊朗与美国上周达成的临时协议存在相互矛盾的解读，穿过该海峡的船只流量也有所放缓。霍尔木兹海峡是全球约五分之一石油和液化天然气供应的过境通道。

航运数据显示，沙特阿美公司周五恢复了在波斯湾拉斯塔努拉终端的原油装运作业，这是全球最大的石油港口，此前该终端已停摆近四个月。

美国国务卿马尔科·鲁比奥在结束海湾地区访问行程时向记者表示，此行旨在安抚对该临时协议感到紧张的地区盟友。他周四称，如果伊朗威胁或封锁海峡船只，“我们将面临严重问题”。

鲁比奥与海湾合作委员会在联合声明中呼吁霍尔木兹海峡实现“自由、无条件且不受限制的航行”，不得收取通行费或“试图宣示控制权”，并表示持久的和平必须解决伊朗的弹道导弹、无人机及其对代理组织的支持问题。

**伊朗警告“敌对与干预政策”**

伊朗外交部周五回应称，美国在海湾地区的军事存在是地区不安全与分裂的根源，并表示根据临时协议条款，该海峡应由德黑兰和阿曼共同管理。

伊朗外交部表示：“我们警告各方不要继续推行敌对和干预性的地区政策。”

今年2月28日，美国和以色列对伊朗发动袭击后，德黑兰有效控制了该航道，导致石油流动中断，全球能源市场和更广泛的经济受到冲击。

台湾长荣海运周五表示，其新加坡籍船舶“长福号”周四在英国海军机构UKMTO推荐的航线上接近阿曼海域时，遭到“不明物体”击中。

该事件未造成人员伤亡，船只随后恢复通行，驶离海峡。

两名美国官员告诉路透社，伊朗向该船开火。伊朗成立的波斯湾海峡管理局——负责管理船只通行霍尔木兹海峡的申请——则表示，通过未经授权航线的后果由“船东、运营方和船长负责”。

美国政府暂未发表评论。美國總統唐納德·特朗普本月早些時候警告，如果伊朗不履行臨時協議（包括重新開放海峽），美國可能會恢復對伊朗的轟炸。

**黎巴嫩、核檢查等爭議焦點**

除了海峽控制權問題外，框架停火協議在其他方面也存在分歧，包括對伊朗的金融激勵、核檢查以及以色列在黎巴嫩的平行戰爭。

該協議設定了60天的談判期來解決更棘手的問題，包括伊朗的核計劃。

在美國，這場戰爭對特朗普構成沉重壓力，距11月的中期選舉已不遠，選舉結果將決定國會的控制權。

國際海事組織——聯合國下屬機構——在阿曼事件後暫停了為船只穿越霍爾木茲海峽提供護航的行動。

國際海事組織和阿曼本週早些時候宣布了一條穿過海峽南部的新航線，以疏散數百艘因戰爭而滯留的船只，此舉激怒了德黑蘭。

韓國總統李在明週五表示，三艘韓國船隻將在本週末離開霍爾木茲海峽，此前海洋部門報告稱又有八艘韓國船只已駛出。

航運數據顯示，兩艘由沙特阿拉伯航運公司Bahri控制的超大型原油運輸船正在拉斯塔努拉裝載原油，另一艘則在附近等待。每艘VLCC可裝載200萬桶石油。

拉斯塔努拉位於沙特阿拉伯東部海岸的波斯灣，位於霍爾木茲海峽以西。衝突前，該終端原油日出口量超過500萬桶。

（巴林馬納姆補充報導；撰稿人加雷斯·瓊斯；編輯艾丹·劉易斯）
articles.summary:
LOADINGERROR LOADING
&

DUBAI/LONDON, June 26 (Reuters) - Iran reasserted its right on Friday to control shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and warned Gulf states against siding with the U.S., a day after an attack on a ship near Oman highlighted the fragility of a preliminary deal to end the Iran war.

Tehran was responding to what it called an “interventionist, irresponsible and provocative” joint statement by the U.S. and six Gulf states that rejected Iran’s insistence that it could charge tolls on vessels transiting the strait.

Advertisement

“Safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz cannot be guaranteed under ambiguous arrangements, parallel routes or decision-making that does not take Iran’s role as a coastal state into account,” Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi said on X.

Oil prices dipped further on Friday, despite conflicting interpretations of last week’s interim deal between Iran and the U.S. and a slowdown in traffic through the strait, where a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas supplies typically passes.

Advertisement

Saudi Aramco resumed crude loadings on Friday at its Ras Tanura terminal in the Gulf, the world’s biggest oil port, after a nearly four-month halt, shipping data showed.

U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio — wrapping up a tour of the Gulf to reassure nervous regional allies about the interim pact — told reporters on Thursday that if Iran threatened or blocked ships in the strait, “we’re going to have a problem.”

In their joint statement, Rubio and the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) called for “free, unconditional, and unrestricted navigation” in the Strait of Hormuz without tolls or “attempts to assert control”, and said a lasting peace must address Iran’s ballistic missiles, drones and support for proxy groups.

Advertisement

Iran Warns Against ‘Hostile And Interventionist Policies’

Iran’s foreign ministry responded on Friday by saying the U.S. military presence in the Gulf was the source of regional insecurity and division, and said the strait should be governed by Tehran and Oman in line with the terms of the interim deal.

“We warn against the continuation of hostile and interventionist policies in the region,” it said.

Tehran took effective control of the waterway after U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran on Feb. 28 triggered the war, disrupting oil flows and rattling global energy markets and the wider economy.

Advertisement

Taiwan’s Evergreen Marine said on Friday its Singapore-flagged ship Ever Lovely had been hit close to Oman on Thursday by an “unknown object” while on a route recommended by the British navy agency UKMTO.

Nobody was hurt in the incident and the ship later resumed its journey out of the strait.

Two U.S. officials told Reuters that Iran had fired on the ship, while Iran’s Persian Gulf Strait Authority — established by Tehran to manage requests for ships to travel through the strait — said passage through unauthorized routes would be “the responsibility of the owner, operator, and vessel commander”.

There was no immediate comment from the U.S. government. U.S. President Donald Trump warned earlier this month that if Iran did not honour the interim deal, including reopening the strait, the U.S. would probably go back to bombing the country.

Advertisement

Lebanon, Nuclear Inspections, Among Points Of Contention

Alongside the issue of control over the strait, disagreements persist over other elements of the framework ceasefire deal, including over financial incentives for Iran, nuclear inspections, and Israel’s parallel war in Lebanon.

The deal has set up 60 days of talks to tackle thornier issues, including Iran’s nuclear program.

In the United States, the war is weighing heavily on Trump ahead of November midterm elections that will determine control of Congress.

Advertisement

The International Maritime Organization, a U.N. agency, temporarily paused its operation to escort ships through the Strait of Hormuz after the Oman incident.

The IMO and Oman had earlier this week announced a new southern route through the strait to evacuate hundreds of ships stranded by the war, angering Tehran.

South Korea’s President Lee Jae Myung said on Friday three South Korean ships would leave the Strait of Hormuz over the weekend after the Oceans Ministry reported eight more South Korean vessels had exited.

Advertisement

Two Very Large Crude Carriers controlled by Saudi Arabia’s shipping arm Bahri were seen loading crude at Ras Tanura, while another waited nearby, shipping data showed. Each VLCC can load 2 million barrels of oil.

Ras Tanura sits on Saudi Arabia’s eastern coast on the Gulf and is west of the Strait of Hormuz. It used to export more than 5 million bpd of crude before the conflict.

(Additional reporting by Gram Slattery in Manama; Writing by Gareth Jones; Editing by Aidan Lewis)
